description: This schematic illustrates the hot rolling and annealing processing schedule for the Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C (wt.%) low-density duplex steel investigated in the work. The y-axis represents temperature in ℃, while the x-axis represents time in h. The process starts with reheating at 1200℃ for 2 h, followed by hot rolling conducted in the temperature range of 900–1100℃. Two post-hot-rolling cooling paths are designed: the solid black line denotes air cooling to ambient temperature, while the dashed red line denotes slower furnace cooling to ambient temperature. Subsequently, both air cooled and furnace cooled samples undergo annealing treatment, with the solid and dashed lines corresponding to the annealing schedules for the two groups of samples respectively. This processing design enables comparative study of the effect of post-rolling cooling path on microstructure evolution and tensile properties of the experimental steel, supporting the core objective of optimizing strength-ductility match of low-density duplex steels via scalable conventional processing routes.

This figure presents microstructure characterization of an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C (wt.%) low-density duplex steel after hot rolling with two different cooling paths. Subfigures (a) and (b) are 10 μm-scale scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs of air-cooled (AC) and furnace-cooled (FC) samples, respectively, showing alternating banded δ-ferrite and austenite-containing phases: the AC sample contains γ+α' (austenite + martensite) bands, while the FC sample has higher austenite fraction with far less martensite. Subfigures (c) and (d) are 50 μm-scale electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) kernel average misorientation (KAM) and image quality (IQ) maps for AC and FC samples, respectively, with black lines marking high-angle grain/phase boundaries (>15°) and red lines marking low-angle grain boundaries (>2°). The AC sample has higher martensite fraction (26 vol% vs 2.8 vol% in FC) and higher austenite KAM value (0.48° vs 0.43° in FC), indicating higher dislocation density from faster cooling-induced martensitic transformation. This figure demonstrates that post-hot-rolling cooling path strongly modulates as-rolled phase fractions, grain boundary features and dislocation density, which directly affect subsequent annealed microstructure and tensile strength-ductility synergy.

This set of 6 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs displays the microstructures of an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel, processed with two post-hot rolling cooling paths (air cooling, AC; furnace cooling, FC) followed by annealing at 800 °C, 900 °C, and 1000 °C. Subplots (a-c) correspond to AC samples annealed at 800 °C, 900 °C, and 1000 °C respectively, while (d-f) correspond to FC samples at the same annealing temperatures, all with 10 μm scale bars. Identified phases include α-ferrite, α' martensite, δ-ferrite, and γ austenite. AC samples exhibit duplex ferrite-austenite microstructures with needle-like α-ferrite refining austenite grains, while FC samples show banded microstructures with coarser austenite grains and a small fraction of martensite. Increasing annealing temperature reduces α-ferrite content in AC samples and promotes recrystallization in both sample groups. This figure confirms that cooling path and annealing temperature effectively tune the steel's microstructure, which underpins the observed difference in mechanical performance.

This figure shows Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps of austenite overlaid on Image Quality (IQ) maps for an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C wt% low-density duplex steel, under different post-hot-rolling cooling paths and annealing temperatures. Subplots (a), (b), (c) correspond to air-cooled (AC) samples annealed at 800 °C, 900 °C, 1000 °C respectively, while (d), (e), (f) correspond to furnace-cooled (FC) samples annealed at the same three temperatures. Red lines mark low-angle grain boundaries, black lines mark high-angle grain boundaries, with a 50 μm scale bar for all subplots. Observed trends include finer austenite grains (3.5–5.8 μm average) in AC samples compared to coarser grains (8.1–10.1 μm average) in FC samples, and decreasing low-angle grain boundary length in AC ferrite with rising annealing temperature indicating progressive recovery. FC samples exhibit banded microstructures with small fractions of martensite in austenite bands. This figure supports that post-hot-rolling air cooling plus annealing refines austenite grains, improves austenite stability, and enables transformation-induced plasticity effect for superior strength-ductility synergy, while furnace cooling leads to poorer ductility due to coarser grains and easy crack nucleation at martensite-austenite interfaces.

This bar chart presents XRD-measured austenite volume fractions of an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C wt% low-density duplex steel before and after tensile deformation, for samples processed with different post-hot-rolling cooling paths and annealing temperatures. X-axis samples include AC800, AC900, AC1000 (air-cooled after hot rolling, annealed at 800, 900, 1000 °C respectively) and FC800, FC900, FC1000 (furnace-cooled after hot rolling, annealed at the same three temperatures). Red bars denote pre-deformation austenite fraction, grey bars denote post-deformation values. AC samples have pre-deformation austenite fractions of 58.6, 61.4, 57.1 vol%, which drop to 23.7, 44.4, 46.4 vol% after deformation, with ~60% austenite consumed in AC800, indicating a strong transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) effect. FC samples have pre-deformation austenite fractions of 62, 58.6, 60.6 vol%, which only drop slightly to 56.5, 52.4, 52.1 vol% after deformation, confirming higher austenite mechanical stability in FC samples. This data supports the core conclusion that the TRIP effect in AC annealed samples drives work hardening rate recovery and improved ductility, while limited austenite transformation in FC samples correlates with lower ductility from interface crack nucleation at pre-existing martensite.

This group contains two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs each with a 10 μm scale bar, presenting microstructures of hot-rolled air-cooled Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel after annealing at 800 °C for different durations. The first micrograph corresponds to the sample annealed for 5 min, which consists of tempered martensite, austenite and δ-ferrite, with clear twin crystal structure in martensite. The second micrograph corresponds to the sample annealed for 15 min, where partial prior martensite degenerates into needle-like α-ferrite, and the austenite films between α-ferrite and/or prior martensite become thickened, indicating increased austenite fraction with longer annealing time. These observations demonstrate the microstructure evolution rule under varied annealing duration, and support the conclusion that the formed needle-like α-ferrite refines austenite grains and enhances austenite mechanical stability, which benefits the good strength-ductility match of the air-cooled annealed steel.

This group of charts presents the austenite grain size distribution of annealed Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C low-density duplex steel samples processed with different post-hot-rolling cooling paths. Subplot (a) corresponds to air-cooled (AC) samples annealed at 800 °C (AC-A800, black curve), 900 °C (AC-A900, red curve), and 1000 °C (AC-A1000, blue curve); subplot (b) corresponds to furnace-cooled (FC) samples annealed at 800 °C (FC-A800, black curve), 900 °C (FC-A900, red curve), and 1000 °C (FC-A1000, blue curve). The y-axis is normalized area proportion of austenite (%), and the x-axis is austenite grain size (μm). AC annealed samples show finer austenite grains, with peak size at ~4 μm, ~6 μm, ~8 μm respectively for the three annealing temperatures, average grain size 3.5, 4.2, 5.8 μm. FC annealed samples have much coarser austenite grains, average grain size 9.3, 8.1, 10.1 μm respectively. Austenite grain size increases with rising annealing temperature for both cooling paths. The finer grains in AC samples improve austenite stability, contributing to better strength-ductility synergy via the TRIP effect during deformation.

This schematic illustrates the microstructural evolution during annealing of Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel, comparing two post-hot-rolling cooling paths: air cooling (AC, upper panel) and furnace cooling (FC, lower panel). For AC samples, the pre-annealing microstructure comprises δ-ferrite, austenite, and α' martensite. During annealing, α' martensite undergoes reverse transformation to austenite and decomposes into needle-like α-ferrite that refines austenite grains, yielding a final annealed microstructure of δ-ferrite, austenite, and α-ferrite, with equilibrium austenite fraction >65% and no post-annealing martensitic transformation. For FC samples, the pre-annealing microstructure contains only δ-ferrite and austenite; during annealing, austenite grains coarsen with only minor equiaxed α-ferrite formation, leading to lower austenite stability, so partial austenite transforms to α' martensite during final cooling, resulting in a 4-phase annealed microstructure. This difference explains the superior strength-ductility synergy of AC annealed samples enabled by stable refined austenite and TRIP effect, versus poorer ductility of FC samples from martensite-induced crack nucleation.

This figure displays engineering tensile stress-strain curves of an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel, comparing samples treated with different post-hot-rolling cooling paths followed by annealing at 800 °C, 900 °C, or 1000 °C. Subplot (a) shows data for air-cooled (AC) samples: as-hot-rolled AC, AC800 (annealed at 800℃), AC900 (annealed at 900℃), and AC1000 (annealed at 1000℃), with engineering strain measured up to 60%. Subplot (b) shows data for furnace-cooled (FC) samples: as-hot-rolled FC, FC800, FC900, FC1000, with engineering strain measured up to 20%. AC annealed samples exhibit significantly higher ductility (all elongations >30%, up to 56.9% for AC800) and balanced strength-ductility performance, enabled by transformation induced plasticity (TRIP) effect that recovers work hardening rate at late deformation stages. FC annealed samples show limited ductility (all elongations <20%, as low as 10.6% for FC1000) and abrupt fracture without necking, caused by crack nucleation at martensite-austenite interfaces and banded microstructure delamination. The figure supports that air cooling after hot rolling produces far superior tensile properties for this duplex steel compared to furnace cooling.

This figure contains two subplots presenting tensile true stress-strain curves and work hardening rate curves of an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C wt% low-density duplex steel after hot rolling, different cooling paths, and annealing at 800 °C, 900 °C, 1000 °C. Subplot (a) shows data for air-cooled (AC) samples (AC800, AC900, AC1000), while subplot (b) shows data for furnace-cooled (FC) samples (FC800, FC900, FC1000). All samples exhibit a rapid drop of work hardening rate in Stage I (dominated by dislocation dynamic recovery), followed by a slow decline in Stage II. AC samples have an extra Stage III with recovered work hardening rate, originating from the transformation induced plasticity (TRIP) effect, leading to high true fracture strain up to ~0.44. FC samples show no Stage III and fracture abruptly at low true strain (max ~0.18), due to crack initiation at martensite and banded microstructure. Fracture strain decreases with rising annealing temperature for both sample groups. This figure confirms that post-hot-rolling air cooling plus annealing delivers a superior strength-ductility match for the studied steel.

This figure shows two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) fracture surface micrographs of Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel after tensile testing, both with a 50 μm scale bar. Subfigure (a) displays the fracture surface of sample AC-A800, which underwent air cooling after hot rolling followed by 800 °C annealing: it is dominated by abundant dimples, a typical feature of ductile fracture, with only minor observed cracking, consistent with its high 56.9% elongation and ductile duplex ferrite-austenite microstructure enabled by the transformation induced plasticity (TRIP) effect. Subfigure (b) shows the fracture surface of sample FC-A800, which underwent furnace cooling after hot rolling followed by 800 °C annealing: it exhibits cleavage brittle fracture features, widespread cracking from banded structure delamination and martensite-austenite interface strain concentration, matching its low 19.0% elongation and abrupt fracture without necking. These distinct fracture morphologies directly confirm that tailoring post-hot-rolling cooling path effectively optimizes the strength-ductility synergy of this duplex steel system.
